message.content:
Research self-hosted open-source platforms as candidate bases for a custom PM system, using CURRENT (2024-2025) web research. Fetch official docs and GitHub. Cite URLs. Be honest about gaps. Evaluate these tools: **NocoDB**, **Baserow**, **Teable** (the three open-source Airtable alternatives), plus **Huly** (huly.io / hcengineering/platform) and **Twenty** (twenty.com, open-source CRM). 

CONTEXT: A consumer-goods licensing company wants to replace ClickUp with a self-hosted system. Their need is heavily a CUSTOM-DATA-MODEL + WORKFLOW problem: custom object types (Project/offer, SKU, Design Collection, Design, Style-numbered product) each with many TYPED custom fields (licensor, property, on-shelf date, a "PI status" enum, Brand Assurance number, cost target, etc.), multi-level relations/hierarchy, custom multi-stage pipelines with stage-transition history (to compute time-in-stage SLAs), a searchable "design library," bulk stage changes, saved per-role views, and ROLE-BASED FIELD-LEVEL visibility (designers see specs but NOT pricing). They also have ~9,000 historical records to migrate and want to keep a separate analytics/AI-query layer fed by webhooks. KEY PRINCIPLE: they don't need it all built-in, as long as they can build 100% of needed functionality ON TOP WITHOUT FORKING (via API/SDK/webhooks/automation/plugins).

For EACH tool (NocoDB, Baserow, Teable, Huly, Twenty), evaluate against these (mark: Built-in / Buildable-without-fork / Requires-fork / Not-possible, with evidence):
- R1 Multiple custom object/entity types (tables/objects).
- R2 Typed custom fields (text/number/date/single-select/multi-select/boolean/relation/link/user, formula/rollup). Richness of field types.
- R3 Multi-level hierarchy / relations (≥3 levels via linked records or tree).
- R4 Custom workflow STATES (single-select status is easy; but is there STATE-TRANSITION HISTORY / an activity/audit log with timestamps accessible via API for time-in-stage computation? Or automation that can log transitions?).
- R5 Bulk operations (multi-select edit) in UI and API.
- R6 Saved filtered/grouped VIEWS (grid/kanban/gallery) — and per-user/per-role views.
- R7 ROLE-BASED access — and crucially any FIELD-LEVEL or per-view permission (hide pricing field from designers). Which of these support field-level/column-level permissions? (Baserow/NocoDB/Teable RBAC granularity; Twenty's permissions; Huly's.)
- R8 Strong search/filter (design library use case).
- R9 File attachment fields via S3/R2; URL/link fields for external thumbnails + NAS paths.
- R10 REST API + WEBHOOKS + automation. SDKs? (NocoDB API + webhooks; Baserow API + webhooks; Teable API + automation; Twenty GraphQL + webhooks; Huly API maturity?)
- R11 EXTENSIBILITY WITHOUT FORKING — plugins/custom UI/automation scripts. (Baserow has a plugin system + self-host; NocoDB plugins; Teable; Twenty's custom objects via no-code + API; Huly is a framework/platform — note its plugin architecture.)
- R12 Self-hostable; LICENSE — be precise and CURRENT for each (NocoDB license?, Baserow MIT-core + premium?, Teable license (AGPL?), Twenty license (AGPL?), Huly license (EPL/AGPL?)). Note any feature-gating of self-host (e.g., RBAC/SSO gated behind paid tiers).
- R13 UX maturity for NON-TECHNICAL users (designers/sales) — would a designer happily use it daily? Does it feel like an app or like a database admin?

Return: (1) a comparison matrix across all 5 tools for R1-R13, (2) the 1-2 strongest candidates as a "build PM on top" base and why, (3) for the strongest: can they build 100% on top without forking, and the biggest risk, (4) which tools are clearly NOT suitable and why, (5) cited URLs. Be especially careful and current on LICENSES and on which features are gated behind paid editions in self-host.

## Licensing for Self-Hosted NocoDB

You can purchase a Business plan license for your self-hosted NocoDB instance through NocoDB Cloud. For full details on permitted and restricted use, refer to the official license text or contact the NocoDB team for commercial licensing options.

## Field-Level Permissions

Table & Field Permissions let you define who can create or delete records in a table, and who can edit values in specific fields. Field permissions apply to API calls and shared forms, restricting the ability to modify field values through these interfaces.

## Webhooks

Recent updates in 2025 include enhanced webhook functionality. Webhook v3 gives you precise control over when and how data changes are pushed to external systems. Additionally, field-level triggers fire webhooks only when specific fields (like Status or Priority) change.

## REST API

NocoDB provides a comprehensive set of REST APIs that allow you to interact programmatically with your data, metadata, and workspace resources, making it possible to integrate NocoDB with external applications, automate workflows, build custom tools, or manage your bases at scale.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Twenty CRM covering all your query topics:

## Overview

Twenty is fully open-source (AGPL-3.0), and is the fastest-growing open-source CRM project in 2026 with 45.4k stars as of 2026-05-01 and $5M seed from November 2024.

## Core Features

Twenty is a full CRM platform with contacts, companies, deal pipelines, tasks, notes, email/calendar sync, role-based permissions, and workflow automation with webhooks. Its key differentiator is a flexible metadata layer where you can create custom objects and fields at runtime, making it adaptable to any sales or ops workflow without code changes.

## Custom Objects and Fields

You can add custom objects and fields from Settings → Data Model without writing code or migrations. Standard objects come pre-configured, but you can create custom objects with any field types you need, and many-to-many relationships between custom objects work as first-class citizens.

## API Capabilities

When you add a custom object (say Invoice), it immediately gets REST and GraphQL endpoints identical to built-in objects, with endpoints using your object and field names directly. The Metadata API at /rest/metadata/ handles schema management to create/modify/delete objects, fields, and relations programmatically.

API keys can be scoped to a specific role under Settings → Members → Roles → Assignment tab to limit what they can access.

## Webhooks

Webhooks fire on object creation, update, and deletion. Webhooks push data to your systems when events occur in Twenty without polling, and when a record is created, updated, or deleted, the platform sends an HTTP POST request to your specified URL with the event details.

## Self-Hosted Deployment

Self-hosting on Railway gives you the full feature set at infrastructure cost only. Twenty pre-wires five services: PostgreSQL, Redis (BullMQ job queue and cache), MinIO (S3-compatible file storage), twenty-server (API + React frontend on port 3000), and twenty-worker (background jobs).

## License Considerations

If you self-host Twenty for internal use only (as most teams do), AGPL does not require you to publish anything, but if you build a SaaS product on top of Twenty and offer it to customers, AGPL applies to your modifications.

input.prompt: Does NocoDB support field-level/column-level permissions to hide a field from certain roles (e.g. hide pricing from designers)? Is this feature free/MIT or gated behind a paid plan? List details and any plan requirements.

List exactly which features are gated behind Baserow's Premium, Advanced, and Enterprise paid self-hosted licenses versus free MIT core. Specifically: role-based permissions, field-level/column permissions, RBAC, SSO, audit log, row history. Which tier is each in?

content.content:
# NocoDB Field Permissions: Not for Field Hiding

Based on the documentation, NocoDB does **not** support hiding fields from specific roles. Instead, field permissions control **edit access only**.

## Key Details

**What field permissions do:**
Field permissions allow you to "control who can edit values in specific fields in a table." However, the documentation explicitly states: "Field permissions do not control field visibility — users with access can still view all field data, but their ability to edit values in specific fields depends on the configured permissions."

**Plan Requirements:**
This feature is gated behind paid plans:
- "Field permissions are available on NocoDB Cloud (Plus plan and above) and licensed self-hosted deployments (Business plan and above)."

## Bottom Line

You cannot hide pricing fields from designers using NocoDB's field permissions. All users can view all fields regardless of their role—you can only restrict who can *edit* specific fields. To achieve visibility controls, you'd need alternative approaches outside this feature.

# Twenty's API Architecture

## API Types

Twenty provides **two complementary APIs**:

1. **Core API** (`/rest/` and `/graphql/`): Handles CRUD operations on records including People, Companies, Opportunities, and custom objects. Supports querying, filtering, and relation traversal.

2. **Metadata API** (`/rest/metadata/` and `/metadata/`): Manages the data model itself—create, modify, or delete objects, fields, and relations programmatically.

## Schema-Per-Tenant Design

Rather than static documentation, each workspace generates its own API based on its schema. When you add custom objects (like `Invoice`), they immediately receive REST and GraphQL endpoints matching built-in objects, using your actual object and field names directly.

## Authentication & API Keys

API keys are created via **Settings → API & Webhooks → + Create key**. They use bearer token authentication:

```
Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY
```

Keys support role-based scoping through **Settings → Members → Roles → Assignment tab**, allowing you to restrict access based on assigned roles.

NocoDB is no longer an open source product, with its licensing approach changed to Fair Code (Sustainable Use License). After versions 0.301.0 and later, NocoDB is no longer published under an OSI-approved license.

The company cited that bad actors take their work and sell it as their own, with no intention of complying with AGPLv3, and engineers have been consulted to help on what appear to be private forks, where code that should be open remains hidden. Bad actors take their work and sell it as their own with no intention of complying with AGPLv3, and engineers have been consulted innumerable times on what appear to be private forks, where code that should be open remains hidden.

## Current License Terms

Under the new Sustainable Use License, the licensor grants a non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide, non-sub-licensable, non-transferable license to use, copy, distribute, make available, and prepare derivative works of the software, subject to limitations, where users may use or modify the software only for their own internal business purposes or for non-commercial or personal use.

## Terminology

The company should be described as "source available" rather than open source, as the new license is not OSI-approved.

Audit details include the performer, IP address, OS and browser info, base, event type, and the full event payload in JSON format.

## Record-Level Revision History

For individual record tracking, the Revision History tab shows every change made to the record, including who made the change and when. Revision history is read-only — you cannot edit or delete entries, and the amount of history retained depends on your workspace plan.

## API Access to Audit Data

While audit logs are available in the UI, there is a feature request to retrieve audit history per table via the API through an endpoint that queries data from the audit table and filters for the relevant table. This suggests that direct API access to audit log data may not currently be fully available as a standard feature.

The Premium license (cheapest paid self-host tier) adds Kanban. I'll flag this. I have sufficient cross-verified evidence across all five tools and all R1-R13 dimensions. Synthesizing now.

---

# Evaluating 5 Self-Hosted Platforms as a "Build-PM-On-Top" Base

**Use case:** A consumer-goods licensing company replacing ClickUp. The core problem is a **custom-data-model + workflow + field-level-permissions** problem, with a hard constraint: *build 100% of needed functionality ON TOP without forking*, plus ~9,000-record migration and a webhook-fed analytics/AI layer.

A note on honesty/gaps: vendor docs are uneven. The most load-bearing and well-corroborated findings are the **licenses** and **which features are gated in self-host**; I flag lower-confidence items inline.

---

## (1) Comparison Matrix (R1–R13)

Legend: **B** = Built-in · **BWF** = Buildable-without-fork · **RF** = Requires-fork · **N** = Not-possible

| # | Requirement | **NocoDB** | **Baserow** | **Teable** | **Twenty** | **Huly** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Multiple custom object types | B | B | B | **B** (custom objects = first-class) | B (Cards/Types) but secondary to fixed apps |
| R2 | Typed custom fields (incl. formula/rollup/relation) | B (rich) | B (rich) | B (rich; Postgres-native) | B (rich, incl. relations) | B but **narrower** field set, no rollup |
| R3 | ≥3-level hierarchy / relations | B (linked records) | B (link-to-table) | B (link fields) | B (many-to-many first-class) | B (Reference + Type inheritance) |
| R4 | **State-transition history via API** (time-in-stage) | Revision history in UI; **audit log Enterprise-only & not cleanly API-exposed** → log transitions yourself via webhooks (BWF) | Row change history in **free** core, but **no documented API read of history** → log via webhooks (BWF) | History exists; API read unclear → webhook-log (BWF) | **Timeline + workflows** can fire on update; **build transition log via webhook/workflow (BWF)** | Activity exists; **webhooks essentially absent (BWF→risky)** |
| R5 | Bulk ops (UI + API) | B UI; API mostly per-row | B UI; bulk via batch endpoints | B UI; bulk API + n8n node | B UI; **batch API up to 60 records/call** | B UI; bulk API immature |
| R6 | Saved grid/kanban/gallery views, per-role | B (kanban free) | Grid/form/gallery **free**; **Kanban/calendar = paid (Premium+)**; view-level perms paid | B (grid/kanban/gallery/calendar/form) | B (table/kanban); CRM-shaped | B (multiple views) |
| R7 | **Field-level visibility RBAC** (hide pricing from designers) | **✗ field perms restrict EDIT only, not visibility** — and paid (Business) | **✓ field-level perms, but Advanced/Enterprise self-host license only** | **✓ "authority matrix", but Business/Enterprise paid only** | **✓ field-level view/edit perms, free CE, shipped v1.4.0 (Aug 2025)** | Weak/unclear field-level visibility |
| R8 | Strong search/filter | B | B | B (Postgres-backed, scales) | B | B but PM-centric |
| R9 | File attachments via S3/R2; URL fields | B (S3) | B (S3) | B (S3/MinIO) | B (MinIO/S3) | B (attachments) |
| R10 | REST API + webhooks + automation; SDK | REST + webhooks B; automation/scripts paid-ish | **REST + webhooks free MIT**; automations paid | REST + webhooks + automation (automation runs metered/paid); n8n node | **REST + GraphQL + Metadata API + webhooks + workflows** (strong) | **WebSocket/TS SDK only; webhooks largely absent** (weak) |
| R11 | Extensibility without forking | Scripts (newer, paid tiers); plugin story weak | **Plugin system + open MIT core** | Plugins + API | **Strongest: runtime custom objects + Metadata API + serverless functions** | Plugin *framework* but extending = developing *in* the platform (fork-like) |
| R12 | Self-host + LICENSE | **⚠ Sustainable Use License (source-available, NOT OSI) since v0.301.0; AGPLv3 only ≤0.300** | **MIT core**; Premium/Advanced/Enterprise paid add-ons | **AGPL-3.0 CE**; EE paid | **AGPL-3.0 CE**; twenty-ee paid | **EPL-2.0** |
| R13 | UX for non-technical users | App-like, polished | App-like, polished | App-like, polished, fast | **Most app-like** (feels like a product, not a DB admin) | Polished but **PM/Linear-shaped**, not a data app |

---

## (2) Strongest Candidates: **Twenty** and **Baserow**

**Twenty (top pick for this specific use case).** It is the only one of the five whose core model *is* "user-defined custom objects with typed fields, each auto-exposing REST + GraphQL endpoints," **plus** field-level permissions that hide fields by role (the make-or-break R7 requirement) — and crucially that field-level permission is in the **free AGPL Community Edition** (shipped v1.4.0, Aug 2025), not gated. It also ships object-level RBAC (v1.0, Jun 2025), workflows with webhooks/HTTP/serverless steps, a Metadata API to create objects/fields programmatically (ideal for scripting the 9,000-record schema + migration), a record timeline, and batch API. Only SSO/SAML and some advanced RBAC sit behind the paid twenty-ee package — not blockers for internal use.

**Baserow (strong, more "spreadsheet-native" pick).** MIT-licensed core, genuine plugin system, unlimited rows/webhooks/API and **row history free** in self-host, rich field types, and a polished non-technical UX. Its catch: the exact feature you need most — **field-level permissions and RBAC — require a paid self-hosted license (Advanced/Enterprise)**, and Kanban/calendar views require the paid Premium tier. So Baserow is excellent *if you pay for at least Advanced*.

---

## (3) Can the top pick (Twenty) build 100% on top without forking? Biggest risk

**Mostly yes.** Custom objects/fields (R1–R3), field-level role visibility (R7), views (R6), attachments (R9), API/GraphQL/webhooks/workflows (R10–R11), and migration via the Metadata + Core APIs (R5) are all **achievable without forking**. The webhook-fed analytics/AI layer is a natural fit (webhooks + GraphQL).

**Biggest risk:** R4 — **stage-transition history for time-in-stage SLAs is not a turnkey feature.** Twenty has a record timeline and "updated by/at," but there is **no formal, API-queryable stage-transition audit log out of the box.** You must *engineer* the transition ledger yourself — e.g., a workflow/webhook that, on every stage-field change, appends a row to a "StageHistory" custom object with timestamps. That's "buildable without forking," but it is custom work you own, and it's the single thing most likely to bite the SLA reporting. Secondary risks: Twenty is **CRM-shaped and fast-moving** (1.x, rapid releases) so deep PM/pipeline ergonomics and stability for non-technical daily users need validation; and AGPL-3.0 means any *externally offered* derivative would trigger copyleft (internal self-host is fine).

*(Baserow has the same R4 "build-your-own-transition-log" risk, plus the added cost gate on R7/RBAC.)*

---

## (4) Clearly NOT suitable

- **Huly — not suitable as a build-on-top base.** It's an opinionated all-in-one **PM/Linear/Notion-style app**, not a data platform. Custom "Cards/Types" exist but with a narrower field set (no rollup), **webhooks are essentially absent** (open feature requests since Nov 2024), the only integration surface is a **WebSocket/TS SDK** (REST only via third-party MCP wrappers), and field-level visibility RBAC is weak. Extending it means *developing inside the platform's plugin framework* — effectively forking-adjacent. It fails the hardest requirements (R4 webhook-driven history, R7 field-level perms, R10/R11 clean API extensibility).

- **NocoDB — disqualified on two independent grounds.** (a) **License:** since **v0.301.0 it is no longer open source** — it moved to a **Sustainable Use License (fair-code, source-available, non-OSI)**; only ≤0.300 is AGPLv3. (b) **R7 fatal gap:** NocoDB's field permissions **control edit access only, not visibility** — *"users with access can still view all field data."* You **cannot hide pricing from designers** with native field permissions (and that feature is paid Business tier anyway). Its audit log is Enterprise-only and not cleanly API-exposed. The exact #1 requirement is unmet.

---

## (5) Cited URLs

**NocoDB**
- License change (no longer OSS, v0.301.0): https://github.com/nocodb/nocodb/discussions/12891 · https://forum.cloudron.io/topic/14918/heads-up-nocodb-is-no-longer-open-source. · https://github.com/nocodb/nocodb/discussions/6741
- Field permissions (edit-only, not visibility; paid): https://nocodb.com/docs/product-docs/roles-and-permissions/field-permissions
- Audit logs (Enterprise-only): https://nocodb.com/docs/product-docs/workspaces/workspace-audit · API gap: https://github.com/nocodb/nocodb/issues/10420
- Webhooks/REST/Roles: https://nocodb.com/docs/product-docs/developer-resources/rest-apis · https://nocodb.com/docs/changelog/2025.06.0

**Baserow**
- MIT core + license tiers: https://baserow.io/user-docs/self-hosted-licenses · https://github.com/baserow/baserow
- Pricing / tier features (RBAC+audit=Advanced; Kanban=Premium): https://baserow.io/pricing
- Field-level perms (Advanced/Enterprise): https://baserow.io/user-docs/field-level-permissions · https://baserow.io/blog/baserow-field-level-permissions-security · https://baserow.io/user-docs/role-based-access-control-rbac
- API/webhooks (free, no self-host limits): https://baserow.io/user-docs/database-api · https://baserow.io/faq

**Teable**
- AGPL-3.0 CE + EE features (authority matrix/automation/admin): https://github.com/teableio/teable · https://help.teable.ai/en/deploy/activate
- Pricing tiers (authority matrix + SSO = Business): https://teable.ai/pricing
- n8n integration node: https://github.com/OfficialMoAdel/n8n-nodes-teable

**Twenty**
- AGPL-3.0 + self-host: https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty · https://twenty.com/
- APIs (REST + GraphQL + Metadata, batch 60): https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/capabilities/apis
- Field-level perms (v1.4.0), RBAC (v1.0), workflows, webhooks, timeline: https://twenty.com/releases · https://docs.twenty.com/user-guide/data-model/capabilities/fields
- SSO gated to paid (twenty-ee): https://use-apify.com/docs/self-hosted/crm-and-gtm/twenty-crm

**Huly**
- EPL-2.0: https://github.com/hcengineering/platform · https://github.com/hcengineering/huly-selfhost/blob/main/LICENSE
- Custom Types/Cards: https://docs.huly.io/cards/types/creating-types/
- API (WebSocket/SDK) + webhook gap: https://docs.huly.io/getting-started/api-tools/ · https://github.com/hcengineering/platform/issues/6996 · https://github.com/hcengineering/platform/issues/7182

---

### Bottom line
- **Build on Twenty** if you want the closest fit to "custom objects + field-level role visibility + clean API/webhooks, free in self-host." Plan to **build the stage-transition ledger yourself** (the one real gap), and validate PM-ergonomics/stability for non-technical daily users.
- **Build on Baserow** if you prefer a spreadsheet-native, MIT-core platform with a plugin system — but **budget for the Advanced self-hosted license** to unlock the field-level RBAC you require.
- **Avoid NocoDB** (license no longer OSS *and* field perms can't hide fields) and **Huly** (fixed PM app, no webhooks, SDK-only) for this build-on-top, field-level-permission-driven use case. Teable is a viable third choice but puts your #1 requirement (authority matrix / field perms) behind the paid Business tier.
